It will directly test competing models for the significance of Neoproterozoic zircon peaks and clarify the link between supercontinent cycles, LIP events, and crustal generation. The results will offer a template for applying similar mass balance approaches to other contentious periods in Earth history.
proposals.novelty: While zircon age peaks are widely discussed, this study uniquely integrates the mafic LIP record—a direct mantle signal—with the felsic zircon isotopic record into a formal mass balance model to solve for crustal growth. This moves beyond correlation-based arguments to a testable, quantitative framework that leverages Condie’s recent work on back-arcs, LIPs, and Proterozoic templates.

proposals.title: Inverting the Crustal Age Spectrum: Linking Episodic Zircon Production to Deep Mantle Convection Regimes
proposals.motivation: The continental crustal record, particularly peaks in zircon U-Pb ages, is widely interpreted as episodic crustal growth, but its ultimate driver remains debated. A key pre-2026 question is whether these peaks are a surface record of deep mantle dynamics, specifically the episodic formation of mantle plumes or large low-shear-velocity provinces (LLSVPs) linked to supercontinent cycles. This proposal directly addresses the challenge of using the crustal archive to constrain deep Earth processes, moving beyond correlation to causation.
proposals.hypothesis: The temporal distribution and geochemical signature of preserved continental crust, especially from large igneous provinces (LIPs) and orogens, can be inverted to infer the timing, location, and thermal flux of major mantle upwelling events (plumes) and the stability of deep mantle geochemical reservoirs throughout the Proterozoic and Archean.
proposals.method: We will compile a global, high-resolution database of igneous and detrital zircon ages (with εHf, δ¹⁸O) and LIP emplacement ages, focusing on known peaks. This crustal 'response function' will be used as an input constraint for 3-D spherical mantle convection models using the CitcomS or ASPECT codes. We will employ an inverse modeling framework, using an adjoint method or a Bayesian Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) approach, to iteratively adjust initial mantle heterogeneity conditions and rheological parameters to find mantle convection histories that best reproduce the observed crustal age and geochemical peaks. The forward model will include a parameterized crustal production module linking specific mantle temperature/upwelling thresholds to melt generation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative, physically grounded framework linking surface geology to deep Earth dynamics, testing models of supercontinent-plume interactions and the long-term evolution of mantle convection. It will move the field from describing episodicity to explaining its mechanism, offering a new tool to interrogate deep time Earth history.
proposals.novelty: While correlations are common, a formal inverse problem approach—using the crustal record to computationally constrain mantle convection parameters—is novel. It integrates Condie's peak analysis with state-of-the-art geodynamic inversion, a synthesis not previously attempted in deep time studies.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Negative Growth Rate: A Zircon-Based Mass Balance for Continental Erosion and Recycling in the Proterozoic
proposals.motivation: The net growth of continental crust is the difference between crustal addition (via magmatism) and crustal destruction (via erosion, sediment recycling, and subduction erosion). While Condie's work on peaks in zircon age spectra and Large Igneous Provinces has illuminated periods of positive growth, the rates and mechanisms of the complementary destructive processes remain poorly quantified. This imbalance limits our understanding of the Precambrian crustal mass balance and its links to supercontinent cycles and mantle dynamics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the temporal distribution of detrital zircon ages in Proterozoic sedimentary basins, when analyzed against the known record of igneous crystallization ages, provides a quantitative proxy for continental erosion and sediment recycling rates, representing periods of 'negative' crustal growth.
proposals.method: We will compile and analyze global U-Pb detrital zircon datasets from well-dated Proterozoic sedimentary successions. Using kernel density estimation (KDE) and Likelihood-Based Proxy (LBP) methods, we will generate a high-resolution record of sediment age populations. This record will be quantitatively compared to the known age distribution of primary igneous zircons (from Condie's database) using cross-correlation and mass-balance modeling. Peaks in the detrital record that lag behind primary magmatic peaks will be interpreted as pulses of erosion and recycling, with the lag time and peak amplitude used to model destruction rates.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first semi-quantitative, time-resolved estimate of continental destruction rates for the Proterozoic, complementing existing models of crustal growth. It will test the hypothesis that wide continental back-arcs were sites of enhanced crustal reworking and identify whether supercontinent assembly or breakup phases were dominant drivers of crustal erosion.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in explicitly framing continental erosion as 'negative growth' and using the detrital zircon record not just as a provenance tool, but as a direct, quantifiable signal of crustal mass loss, creating a dual framework to the established study of crustal addition.

proposals.title: Quantifying Hadean to Paleoarchean Crustal Volume via Lu-Hf and Sm-Nd Isotope Systematics in Preserved Mafic Crust
proposals.motivation: The volume and growth rate of continental crust in the first billion years of Earth's history remain poorly constrained, directly impacting models of early tectonics, mantle evolution, and planetary habitability. Existing estimates from the detrital zircon record are model-dependent and lack a direct link to mantle depletion. This project addresses this gap by applying a mantle depletion proxy to the oldest preserved rock record.
proposals.hypothesis: The Hf/Sm ratio of early Archean (4.0-3.2 Ga) non-arc basalts, corrected for mantle source heterogeneity using coupled Lu-Hf and Sm-Nd isotopes, provides a quantitative measure of prior continental crust extraction, revealing a more significant volume of pre-3.5 Ga crust than indicated by the surviving rock record.
proposals.method: We will compile and analyze a global database of high-precision Lu-Hf and Sm-Nd isotope data from well-characterized, least-altered early Archean basalts and komatiites. Using a mass balance algorithm, we will calculate the time-integrated depletion of their mantle sources, expressed as the deviation of measured Hf/Sm from the primitive mantle value. This deviation will be translated into crustal extraction mass using published partition coefficients and a Monte Carlo simulation to propagate uncertainties from source composition, melting degree, and secondary alteration.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first rock-based, quantitative estimate of continental crust volume extant in the Hadean to Paleoarchean, offering a critical ground-truth for zircon-based models. It will directly test competing hypotheses of rapid early crustal growth versus gradual, protracted growth, with implications for the onset of subduction and supercontinent cycles.
proposals.novelty: While Hf-Nd isotopes are commonly used qualitatively, this study innovates by rigorously applying the Hf/Sm ratio as a *quantitative* barometer for crustal mass, integrating it with Condie's expertise in crustal age peaks and large igneous provinces to frame the results within a global geodynamic context.

proposals.title: Deconvolving the Mantle: A Signal Processing Approach to Isolate the Early Enriched Reservoir from the Global Igneous Record
proposals.motivation: The existence and nature of a deep, early enriched mantle reservoir (e.g., FOZO, C) is a central but unresolved problem in mantle geochemistry, critical for understanding Earth's first billion years of differentiation. Current models are confounded by the overprinting effects of later crustal recycling, mantle mixing, and the convolution of multiple source components in erupted magmas, particularly across the mid-Proterozoic magmatic lull and peaks identified in the detrital zircon record.
proposals.hypothesis: The global geochemical signal of mantle-derived rocks can be mathematically deconvolved using known end-member compositions, allowing for the isolation and precise characterization of a unique, early enriched component distinct from recycled crustal materials and the depleted MORB mantle.
proposals.method: We will compile a global pre-2026 database of high-precision isotopic (Nd, Hf, Pb) and trace element data from well-characterized, ancient mantle-derived igneous rocks (komatiites, basalts) and their zircons. Using a non-negative matrix factorization (NMF) algorithm—an unsupervised method for blind source separation—we will decompose the multi-dimensional geochemical array into its constituent end-members. This will be coupled with a Bayesian inverse modeling framework to quantify mixing proportions and assess uncertainties, treating the data as a convoluted signal where the 'sources' are the mantle reservoirs.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative, data-driven definition of the early enriched mantle's isotopic identity and its volumetric contribution through time. It will directly test models linking mantle heterogeneity to supercontinent cycles and offer a new template for interpreting secular changes in crustal growth peaks and the composition of continental back-arcs.
proposals.novelty: The application of formal blind source separation techniques from signal processing represents a significant methodological departure from conventional mixing hyperbola or simple binary mixing models used in geochemistry. It systematically addresses the 'unmixing' problem inherent in mantle petrology without *a priori* assumptions about the number or purity of contributing reservoirs.

proposals.title: Quantifying Mantle Heterogeneity Through Time: A Hierarchical Bayesian Joint Inversion of Global Basalt Geochemistry
proposals.motivation: The geochemical record of continental and oceanic magmatism, particularly the peaks and lulls in zircon age spectra, is interpreted through the lens of mantle source heterogeneity and plate tectonic processes. However, existing mixing models for mantle end-members (DMM, EM, HIMU) rely on pre-defined source compositions, introducing prior bias and obscuring the true evolution of mantle reservoirs through Earth history. A statistically rigorous, data-driven method is needed to independently solve for both source signatures and mixing proportions across the geologic timescale.
proposals.hypothesis: A hierarchical Bayesian joint inversion of a global primitive basalt database will reveal that the number and geochemical identity of mantle end-members, and their proportional contributions to crust generation, have varied systematically in response to supercontinent cycles and the evolution of plate tectonics, as recorded in peaks of continental crust formation.
proposals.method: We will implement a Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampling scheme within a hierarchical Bayesian framework to jointly invert isotopic (Sr, Nd, Hf, Pb) and trace element data from a curated global database of primitive basalts (pre-2026, e.g., GEOROC, PetDB). The model parameters will include the isotopic/trace element compositions of N mantle end-members and their mixing proportions for each sample. Weakly informative, physically bounded priors (e.g., based on known meteorite analogues and extreme mantle compositions) will be used for end-members, with model comparison (e.g., WAIC, Bayes factors) to determine the optimal number (N) of components. The inversion will be applied to time-sliced datasets corresponding to key tectonic phases.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative, probabilistic estimates of mantle source compositions and their mixing proportions through time, directly linking mantle dynamics to the continental crustal record. It will offer a new template for interpreting global geochemical databases, reducing interpretive bias, and testing hypotheses about the relationship between supercontinent amalgamation, large igneous province (LIP) genesis, and mantle reservoir evolution.
proposals.novelty: Unlike conventional fixed-end-member mixing models, this approach simultaneously solves for both the identity of mantle components and their contributions, formally quantifying uncertainty and allowing the data to constrain the permissible range of end-member compositions within a global inversion framework.

proposals.title: Unsupervised Machine Learning of Archean-Proterozoic Basalt Geochemistry Reveals the Secular Evolution of Mantle Source Reservoirs
proposals.motivation: The geochemical transition in basalts around 2 Ga, marked by a pronounced shift in Nb/Th ratios, is a key but enigmatic feature in Earth's secular evolution. It potentially signals a fundamental change in mantle dynamics, crust-mantle interaction, or the influence of deep-seated reservoirs like a maturing subcontinental lithospheric mantle. Resolving whether this change is abrupt or gradual, and identifying the specific mantle components involved, is critical for understanding the co-evolution of the crust, mantle, and plate tectonics through the Proterozoic.
proposals.hypothesis: The application of unsupervised machine learning to a global, temporally-resolved basalt database will reveal that the 2 Ga geochemical shift reflects the changing prevalence of several distinct, statistically robust mantle source components, rather than a simple binary switch, tracking the progressive differentiation and sampling of the mantle during supercontinent assembly.
proposals.method: We will compile a global database of Archean to Paleoproterozoic basalt geochemistry, rigorously filtered for alteration and including key immobile trace elements (e.g., Nb, Th, Zr, Y, REEs). High-dimensional data will be processed using non-negative matrix factorization (NMF) and variational autoencoders (VAEs) to decompose the geochemical space into interpretable end-members without a priori petrogenetic models. The temporal evolution of these end-member contributions will be quantified by projecting basalt compositions from successive time bins onto the learned components, directly testing the nature of the 2 Ga transition.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first data-driven, quantitative deconvolution of secular mantle source evolution across the Archean-Proterozoic boundary. It will directly test competing models for the 2 Ga shift and characterize the geochemical identity of the contributing mantle reservoirs. The resulting template of mantle source evolution will serve as a critical constraint for geodynamic models of early Earth differentiation and supercontinent cycles.
proposals.novelty: While secular curves are common, applying unsupervised machine learning to discover the underlying mantle components from the basalt record itself, free from model-based assumptions, is a novel and powerful approach. This method can reveal previously unrecognized reservoirs and quantify their mixing relationships through time in a way traditional ratio-based studies cannot.

proposals.title: Geochemical Fingerprinting of Plume-Plateau Systems: Hf/Sm and Zr/Nb Ratios in Modern Oceanic Plateaus as Proxies for Archean Continental Nuclei
proposals.motivation: The geodynamic setting of Earth's earliest continental crust remains debated, with plume-related magmatism on oceanic plateaus posited as a key precursor. However, robust geochemical discriminants for ancient plume-derived crust are needed. Modern large igneous provinces (LIPs), like the Ontong Java and Caribbean plateaus, provide accessible analogs for testing such discriminants within a known plate tectonic context.
proposals.hypothesis: Modern oceanic plateaus, formed by plume magmatism, exhibit diagnostic ranges of Hf/Sm and Zr/Nb ratios that are distinct from mid-ocean ridge and arc settings. These same geochemical signatures can be identified in Archean tonalite-trondhjemite-granodiorite (TTG) suites, supporting a plume-plateau origin for the nuclei of the first continents.
proposals.method: We will compile a global geochemical database for well-characterized modern oceanic plateaus (e.g., Ontong Java, Caribbean, Kerguelen), focusing on immobile trace element ratios Hf/Sm and Zr/Nb. This dataset will be statistically compared to global datasets for mid-ocean ridge basalts (MORB) and island arc basalts using principal component analysis and discriminant function analysis to define a 'plume plateau' field. These modern fingerprints will then be applied to a curated global compilation of Archean (4.0-2.5 Ga) TTG and greenstone belt geochemistry, assessing the proportion that plots within the defined plume field versus arc-like compositions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative, trace-element-based template for identifying plume signatures in the ancient rock record, directly testing the 'plume-plateau' model for continental genesis. It will refine our understanding of the relative roles of plumes versus subduction in the Archean, contributing to the ongoing debate on the onset of modern plate tectonics and the formation of supercontinent nuclei.
proposals.novelty: While Hf-Nd isotopes are commonly used, the combined application of the immobile and fractionation-resistant ratios Hf/Sm (a proxy for garnet control) and Zr/Nb (indicative of source enrichment) as a dual proxy system for ancient plume magmatism is novel. This approach bypasses the isotopic resetting issues common in Archean rocks, offering a fresh test of Condie's 'peaks' in crustal growth and their link to mantle plumes.

proposals.title: Fossil Fluid Pathways as Proxies for Paleo-Heat Flow: Integrating Magnetotelluric Imaging and Geodynamic Models to Constrain Mid-Proterozoic Tectonics
proposals.motivation: The mid-Proterozoic (1.8–0.8 Ga) is characterized by enigmatic peaks in global zircon age distributions and protracted, high-temperature metamorphism, features not easily explained by modern-style plate tectonics. Pre-2026 models, including Condie's 'hot, wide back-arcs' hypothesis, propose unique mantle thermal regimes and crustal dynamics, but lack direct spatial constraints on past heat and fluid transport. Testing these models requires moving beyond the indirect geochemical record to identify physical evidence of ancient thermal and metasomatic processes.
proposals.hypothesis: That crustal-scale electrical conductivity anomalies, imaged in ancient cratons, represent fossilized fluid pathways and metasomatic fronts, and their 3D geometry and distribution provide a quantitative record of paleo-heat flow that can discriminate between competing geodynamic models for the Proterozoic.
proposals.method: We will conduct high-resolution magnetotelluric (MT) surveys across key Proterozoic terrains (e.g., the Grenville Province, SW USA). The 3D inversion of MT data will use the ModEM algorithm to resolve conductivity structures. These anomalies, interpreted as graphitic/sulfide networks or hydrated minerals from paleo-hydrothermal systems, will be digitized as spatial point/line datasets. These will serve as ground-truth constraints for coupled geochemical-geodynamic models (built using the ASPECT or CitcomS code) that simulate mantle convection, melt generation, and fluid release in proposed mid-Proterozoic settings (e.g., wide back-arcs, stagnant-lid drips). Model-predicted thermal gradients and fluid flux patterns will be statistically compared to the imaged anomaly networks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, spatially explicit test of geodynamic models for the mid-Proterozoic 'boring billion'. It will establish a novel methodology for using geophysical imaging to calibrate deep-time tectonic models. The results will directly inform debates on the tempo of supercontinent assembly (Nuna/Rodinia) and the evolution of continental lithosphere by linking preserved physical structures to mantle processes.
proposals.novelty: The integration of present-day geophysical snapshots (MT) as quantitative input for paleo-geodynamic models is a significant departure from standard approaches that rely solely on geochemistry or geology. It directly tests the spatial predictions of tectonic models like the wide back-arc hypothesis against a 3D physical record.

proposals.title: Geodynamic Drivers of Heterogeneous Mid-Proterozoic Ocean Chemistry: A Model Linking Mantle Dynamics, Continental Configuration, and Basin Stratification
proposals.motivation: The mid-Proterozoic (1.8–0.8 Ga) is characterized by enigmatic geochemical and biological stasis, often attributed to a globally uniform, sulfidic ocean. However, this 'boring billion' paradigm is increasingly challenged by localized geochemical proxies. A non-uniform ocean model is required to reconcile global-scale tectonic reconstructions with disparate local sedimentary records, particularly within the context of prolonged supercontinent Nuna/Columbia assembly and the prevalence of wide, hot continental back-arcs.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the mid-Proterozoic ocean was chemically heterogeneous, with strong basin-scale stratification and redox gradients, driven by the unique mantle dynamics, continental configurations, and the widespread development of semi-restricted, volcanically active back-arc basins.
proposals.method: We will integrate a forward geochemical box model with a plate tectonic reconstruction framework for the 1.8–1.0 Ga interval. First, we will use Condie's LIP and orogen databases to define volcanic and hydrothermal flux inputs spatially and temporally. Second, we will employ a modified COPSE biogeochemical model, adapted to simulate multiple interconnected basins (e.g., open ocean, restricted inland seas, back-arcs) with variable circulation thresholds. Key model parameters will be constrained by a compiled global database of Proterozoic iron formations, shale trace metal (Mo, U, V) concentrations, and sulfur isotopes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first dynamically linked framework connecting mantle-driven tectonics (LIPs, back-arcs) to heterogeneous ocean chemistry, moving beyond the single-basin paradigm. It will offer testable predictions for sedimentary geochemistry in different paleo-basins and clarify the environmental context for mid-Proterozoic biological evolution.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in explicitly modeling ocean heterogeneity as a direct consequence of the unique plate tectonic regime of the mid-Proterozoic—specifically the hot, wide continental back-arcs—rather than treating the ocean as a chemically uniform reservoir, which is the standard assumption in existing models.

proposals.title: Crustal Filtering of a Uniform Mantle: Testing the Role of Assimilation in Generating Post-3 Ga Magmatic Diversity
proposals.motivation: The post-3 Ga geological record is marked by a dramatic increase in the diversity of igneous rock compositions, particularly in continental settings. This diversification is commonly linked to the onset of modern-style plate tectonics and mantle heterogeneity. However, the extent to which this diversity can be generated solely through the interaction of a relatively uniform mantle melt with a newly stabilized and compositionally heterogeneous continental crust remains a critical, untested question. Resolving this is key to understanding the coupled evolution of the crust and mantle and the validity of using crustal rocks as direct mantle probes.
proposals.hypothesis: The observed diversity in post-3 Ga continental magmas can be generated primarily through bulk assimilation and fractional crystallization (AFC) of a uniform, depleted mantle melt (e.g., a common tholeiitic basalt) with pre-existing, compositionally diverse crustal lithologies, without requiring significant mantle source heterogeneity.
proposals.method: We will employ a large-scale geochemical modeling approach using a comprehensive, pre-2026 global igneous rock database (e.g., EarthChem). A Monte Carlo simulation algorithm will be developed to stochastically model AFC processes. The model will start with a single, defined primitive mantle melt composition and assimilate a wide range of potential crustal endmembers (derived from global databases of Archean TTG, sedimentary, and older mafic crust compositions). The outputs will be statistically compared to the actual distribution of post-3 Ga igneous rock compositions in multi-element space (e.g., rare earth elements, incompatible trace elements). Model success will be evaluated by the degree of overlap between the simulated compositional arrays and the natural data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative, process-based framework for deconvolving crustal vs. mantle signals in ancient magmatic suites. It will directly test the 'crustal filter' hypothesis for continental magmatism and offer a null model against which evidence for true mantle heterogeneity must be judged. The results will refine interpretations of zircon Hf-O isotopic peaks and the magmatic record within the supercontinent cycle.
proposals.novelty: While AFC is a well-known process, systematically testing it as the *primary* engine for global-scale magmatic diversification over billions of years, using a uniform mantle source, is a novel and provocative approach. It challenges the prevailing paradigm that interprets magmatic diversity as a direct reflection of mantle compositional variation.

proposals.title: Sedimentary Provenance and Detrital Zircon Age Spectra as Primary Archives of Orogenic and Back-arc Evolution in the Proterozoic
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 tectonic models for ancient orogenic belts heavily rely on the geochemistry and ages of volcanic and plutonic rocks to infer tectonic settings and magmatic pulses. However, volcanic sequences are often discontinuous and subject to preservation bias. Sedimentary units, particularly their detrital zircon age spectra and provenance signatures, offer a more continuous, high-fidelity record of crustal evolution, supercontinent cycles, and the nature of wide continental back-arcs proposed for the mid-Proterozoic.
proposals.hypothesis: The temporal and compositional evolution of sedimentary basins, recorded through integrated detrital zircon U-Pb-Hf-O isotopes and whole-rock geochemistry, provides a more fundamental and complete template for reconstructing Proterozoic orogenic belt evolution than the episodic volcanic record alone.
proposals.method: We will compile and analyze existing and new detrital zircon U-Pb-Hf-O isotopic data from well-characterized Proterozoic sedimentary sequences globally, focusing on key intervals (e.g., 2.0-1.8 Ga, 1.3-1.0 Ga). This database will be interrogated using kernel density estimation and mixture modeling to deconvolve age peaks and provenance sources. The sedimentary record will be compared to the contemporaneous igneous record (e.g., LIPs, arc magmatism) using time-series analysis to test for lead-lag relationships and preservation gaps. Tectonic interpretations will be framed within the 'hot, wide back-arc' and supercontinent assembly models.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a sediment-centric framework for testing models of Proterozoic tectonics, potentially recalibrating the timing and drivers of crustal growth pulses. It will establish whether sedimentary basins are the primary integrators of orogenic processes, offering a more robust basis for subdividing the pre-Cryogenian timescale and understanding the links between orogenesis, supercontinents, and mantle dynamics.
proposals.novelty: While detrital zircon studies are common, this proposal explicitly challenges the primacy of the igneous record in tectonic reconstructions, positioning sedimentary systems as the fundamental dataset. It integrates Condie's concepts of age peaks and supercontinent cycles with a novel, basin-focused analytical approach.

proposals.title: Forward-Modeling Stratigraphic Architecture from Tectonic Templates: Testing the Hot, Wide Back-arc Model for the Mid-Proterozoic
proposals.motivation: The mid-Proterozoic (1.8-0.8 Ga) is characterized by a 'boring billion' magmatic and metamorphic record, with subdued peaks in zircon age spectra and a paucity of major orogenic belts. Recent tectonic models, such as the 'hot, wide continental back-arc' hypothesis, offer a geodynamic explanation for this enigma. However, a key test of such models lies in their predicted sedimentary record, which remains underexplored, creating a gap between tectonic theory and stratigraphic observation.
proposals.hypothesis: The stratigraphic architecture (thickness, facies distribution, unconformity frequency, and provenance signals) predicted by a forward model based on a hot, wide back-arc tectonic template will match the observed stratigraphic patterns in well-preserved mid-Proterozoic basins better than models based on modern-style plate boundary configurations.
proposals.method: We will develop a process-based forward stratigraphic model (using a modified version of the open-source 'Badlands' code) that translates tectonic subsidence/uplift, sediment supply, and base-level changes into 3D stratigraphic architectures. Input tectonic templates will be derived from Condie's back-arc model, specifying thermal subsidence rates, hinterland uplift widths, and volcanic input. The model will be calibrated against and tested using the detailed stratigraphy of selected cratonic basins (e.g., Belt-Purcell, McArthur). Key outputs will be synthetic stratigraphic columns, basin geometries, and detrital zircon age patterns for comparison with the rock record.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative, testable link between a novel geodynamic model for a problematic era and its observable stratigraphic consequences. It will move beyond qualitative correlation of age peaks to generate falsifiable predictions for basin evolution. The resulting framework can serve as a template for interpreting other poorly understood Precambrian stratigraphic successions within a specific tectonic context.
proposals.novelty: While forward stratigraphic modeling is established, its application is novel in the context of testing non-uniformitarian, Precambrian tectonic models like the hot back-arc. This approach inverts the standard practice of inferring tectonics from stratigraphy, instead using a theoretical tectonic driver to predict stratigraphy.

proposals.title: Re-evaluating Neoproterozoic Crustal Growth: A Geochemical and Statistical Test of Two-Stage Nd Model Ages as Mixing Artifacts
proposals.motivation: The two-stage Nd model age (T_DM2) is a cornerstone for interpreting crustal evolution, with a prominent peak at 0.6-0.73 Ga often cited as evidence for significant Neoproterozoic crustal growth. However, this interpretation is at odds with the well-established peak in the detrital zircon record at ~1.1-1.2 Ga and the relative magmatic quiescence of the mid-Proterozoic. This discrepancy challenges our understanding of continental growth patterns and the reliability of a key geochemical proxy.
proposals.hypothesis: The 0.6-0.73 Ga T_DM2 peak is not a primary signal of Neoproterozoic juvenile crust formation but is instead a mathematical artifact generated by the mixing of older crustal components with mantle-derived magmas in specific tectonic settings, particularly wide continental back-arcs.
proposals.method: We will compile a global geochemical database of Neoproterozoic to early Paleozoic igneous rocks with reported T_DM2 ages. Using a non-negative matrix factorization (NMF) algorithm, we will deconvolve the measured Nd isotopic compositions into potential end-member components. We will then employ forward mixing models, constrained by trace element ratios (e.g., Th/Nb, La/Sm), to test if the observed T_DM2 distribution can be reproduced by mixing between a depleted mantle source and known Archean/Paleoproterozoic crustal reservoirs. Spatial analysis will correlate mixing trends with tectonic reconstructions of back-arcs and orogens.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, quantitative framework for interpreting Nd model ages, moving beyond simple two-stage assumptions. It will reconcile the Nd and zircon records by linking the T_DM2 peak to a specific tectonic process (back-arc mixing) rather than a crustal growth event, refining models of Proterozoic plate tectonics and continental evolution.
proposals.novelty: While mixing is acknowledged as a potential complication, this study is the first to systematically apply multivariate statistical decomposition and targeted geochemical modeling to test if the global T_DM2 peak is predominantly a mixing signal, directly integrating this with the back-arc tectonic model for the Proterozoic.

proposals.title: Probing Post-Eocene Mantle Heterogeneity: A Zircon Hf-Isotope Test for the Persistence of Enriched Domains in Continental Back-arcs
proposals.motivation: The secular evolution of the mantle is a key constraint on crustal growth models, often depicted as a monotonic depletion trend. However, Condie's recent work on wide, hot Proterozoic back-arcs suggests such settings can generate and preserve enriched mantle signatures. Testing whether similar, long-lived enriched domains persisted into the Cenozoic, particularly post-Eocene, challenges the assumption that younger magmatic records are universally dominated by depleted mantle signals, with implications for understanding mantle-crust coupling in modern tectonic settings.
proposals.hypothesis: That specific, tectonically stable continental regions, particularly those with a history of back-arc or intraplate magmatism, host mantle domains that remained locally enriched (as recorded by juvenile crust) well after the Eocene, and that the global mantle depletion trend is an overprint that masks significant lateral heterogeneity.
proposals.method: We will compile a global, age-filtered database of igneous zircon Hf-isotope data (εHf) from magmatic rocks aged 40-0 Ma, focusing on regions with pre-existing enriched crust (e.g., Proterozoic terrains). Using spatial analysis and kernel density estimation, we will map εHf values against tectonic domains (cratons, orogens, back-arcs). We will apply a novel 'mantle inversion' algorithm that statistically deconvolves the crustal residence age component from the juvenile magmatic εHf signal to estimate the εHf of the mantle source at the time of melt extraction, testing for anomalously enriched values.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a high-resolution, rock-based test for the spatial and temporal persistence of enriched mantle domains into the late Cenozoic. It will directly link Condie's models of ancient crustal production in back-arcs to modern mantle geochemistry, offering a new framework for interpreting zircon Hf datasets in a plate tectonic context and refining models of supercontinent cycle influence on mantle heterogeneity.
proposals.novelty: Instead of using the mantle record to explain crustal age peaks, this inverts the approach by using a detailed crustal isotopic record (zircon εHf) to interrogate the survival of ancient mantle heterogeneity, specifically testing the longevity of enriched domains predicted by the hot back-arc model beyond the Proterozoic.

proposals.title: Beyond Crustal Contamination: Quantifying the Roles of Magma Mixing and AFC in Generating Continental Crust Geochemical Signatures
proposals.motivation: Geochemical variations in continental igneous rocks are frequently attributed to crustal contamination of mantle-derived melts. However, this paradigm may oversimplify complex magmatic processes. A more nuanced understanding is critical for accurately interpreting zircon age peaks, crustal growth models, and the petrogenetic record of large igneous provinces (LIPs) and orogens within the supercontinent cycle.
proposals.hypothesis: For key Proterozoic and Phanerozoic magmatic suites, particularly those associated with wide continental back-arcs and LIPs, geochemical trends traditionally ascribed to crustal contamination can be better explained by combined processes of magma mixing and assimilation-fractional crystallization (AFC).
proposals.method: We will compile a global geochemical database of igneous rocks from targeted age intervals (e.g., mid-Proterozoic, Pan-African). Using quantitative trace element and isotopic modeling (e.g., energy-constrained AFC, magma mixing equations), we will test the ability of pure contamination vs. hybrid AFC/mixing models to reproduce observed trends. Statistical analysis of zircon Hf-O isotopes from associated detrital records will provide independent constraints on crustal reworking vs. juvenile addition.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a revised framework for interpreting the petrogenesis of continental crust, moving beyond a binary contaminated/juvenile dichotomy. It will offer improved explanations for geochemical diversity in the rock record, directly impacting models of crustal evolution and the interpretation of peaks in the detrital zircon record within the context of supercontinent assembly and breakup.
proposals.novelty: While AFC and mixing are known processes, their systematic quantification as primary alternatives to simple contamination for explaining major crustal geochemical signatures, particularly in the context of Condie's identified tectonic settings (hot, wide back-arcs), represents a significant shift in interpretive focus.

proposals.title: Crustal Contaminants in Continental Magmatism: Identifying the Source of OIB-like Signatures in Proterozoic Igneous Provinces
proposals.motivation: Ocean Island Basalt (OIB)-like geochemical signatures in continental magmas are often attributed to plume-derived melts. However, Condie's recent work on wide Proterozoic back-arcs and the supercontinent cycle suggests that crustal recycling processes, including subduction erosion and sediment underplating, may generate deep crustal reservoirs with OIB-like isotopic and trace element characteristics. Identifying these crustal sources is crucial for accurately interpreting the mantle vs. crustal contributions to continental growth and for testing models of Proterozoic tectonic regimes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that OIB-type geochemical signatures in continental magmas, particularly in mid-Proterozoic large igneous provinces (LIPs) and within-plate granitoids, are not solely plume-derived but can be generated by the partial melting of or re-melting of specific, previously subduction-modified crustal materials stored in the deep continental lithosphere.
proposals.method: We will compile a global geochemical database (pre-2026) of Proterozoic igneous rocks with OIB affinities, focusing on Condie's identified age peaks. Using a multi-proxy approach, we will apply principal component analysis (PCA) and unsupervised clustering algorithms to distinguish between plume-like and crustal-like OIB signatures. We will then employ forward and inverse geochemical modeling (e.g., MELTS, trace element mixing models) to test if the 'crustal OIB' signatures can be reproduced by melting known crustal end-members, such as subducted oceanic plateaus, pelagic sediments, or metasomatized lower crust, whose presence is implied by the wide back-arc template.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new framework for deconvolving mantle and crustal sources in continental magmatism, directly testing the role of crustal recycling in generating 'apparent' plume signals. It will refine models of Proterozoic crustal evolution by linking specific magmatic events to deep crustal reservoirs formed during earlier subduction and assembly phases, offering a tangible mechanism for the 'enigmatic' mid-Proterozoic record.
proposals.novelty: While OIB signatures are typically sought in the mantle, this proposal explicitly investigates the crust as a potential source, integrating Condie's tectonic models (back-arcs, supercontinents) with a systematic geochemical data mining strategy to identify crustal contaminants with OIB-like fingerprints.

proposals.title: Integrating Mantle Evolution and Crustal Growth: A Geodynamic Model for the Assembly of East Asia from the Proterozoic to the Phanerozoic
proposals.motivation: The tectonic evolution of East Asia is a complex amalgamation of multiple continental blocks, but the geodynamic drivers of its assembly, particularly the role of deep mantle processes, remain poorly understood. Recent work on global peaks in crustal growth, supercontinent cycles, and the significance of large igneous provinces (LIPs) provides a new temporal framework for linking mantle evolution to surface tectonics. This proposal aims to test whether the episodic mantle dynamics recorded in global zircon and LIP age spectra can explain the specific timing and style of continental accretion and magmatism in East Asia.
proposals.hypothesis: The episodic assembly of East Asia, including the suturing of the North and South China Cratons and the accretion of the Central Asian Orogenic Belt, was fundamentally paced and modulated by long-wavelength mantle convection patterns linked to global supercontinent cycles and LIP events.
proposals.method: We will develop a series of 2D and 3D regional geodynamic models using the finite-element code ASPECT, constrained by pre-2026 mantle evolution proxies. Inputs will include time-dependent plate boundary conditions from paleogeographic reconstructions and spatio-temporal maps of mantle potential temperature and composition derived from the global LIP and detrital zircon age databases. The models will incorporate visco-plastic rheologies to simulate lithospheric deformation, with specific subduction parameters tuned to replicate the wide, hot back-arc systems hypothesized for the mid-Proterozoic. Model outputs will be tested against the observed geological record of East Asia, including the ages of magmatic peaks, metamorphic events, and continental collisions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative, mantle-driven geodynamic framework for the Phanerozoic assembly of East Asia, directly linking regional tectonics to global-scale Earth evolution patterns. It will test the applicability of the 'hot, wide back-arc' model to specific ancient orogens and offer a mechanistic explanation for the observed peaks in East Asian continental growth ages.
proposals.novelty: While Condie's work has identified global peaks and patterns, this project uniquely scales these insights down to a specific, complex region using advanced numerical modeling, moving from correlation to causative geodynamic mechanism. It integrates mantle evolution proxies as direct inputs into a regional tectonic model, a synthesis not previously attempted for East Asia.

proposals.title: Inverting Magmatic Compositions to Reveal Hidden Crust: A Geochemical Approach to Constraining Assimilated Continental End-Members in Proterozoic Magmatic Provinces
proposals.motivation: The composition of the continental crust through time is a primary record of Earth's geodynamic evolution, yet its ancient, unexposed portions remain cryptic. Standard models of crustal growth and evolution rely heavily on the preserved rock record, which is biased by erosion and tectonic recycling. This project addresses a key gap by developing a method to infer the composition of crust that was assimilated into magmas but is no longer directly accessible, providing a novel window into 'missing' crustal reservoirs, particularly during enigmatic periods like the mid-Proterozoic.
proposals.hypothesis: The geochemical signature of assimilated crustal end-members can be quantitatively recovered by inverting the major and trace element, and isotopic (e.g., Nd, Hf) compositions of contaminated mantle-derived magmas, using mass balance and mixing models constrained by known mantle melt compositions.
proposals.method: We will employ an inverse Monte Carlo simulation framework. Starting with a database of well-characterized, potentially contaminated igneous rocks (e.g., from Proterozoic large igneous provinces and continental arcs), we will use forward assimilation-fractional crystallization (AFC) models to generate a suite of possible contaminated melt compositions. The inverse algorithm will then iteratively test millions of potential assimilant compositions (major elements, trace elements, isotopic ratios) against the observed data, using a likelihood function to identify the most probable crustal end-member. This will be implemented using pre-2026 tools like the Geochemical Data Toolkit (GCDkit) and custom Python scripts leveraging libraries such as NumPy and SciPy for optimization.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new methodological template for extracting hidden crustal information from the magmatic record, moving beyond descriptive mixing trends. It will yield testable, quantitative estimates of assimilated crust composition for key Proterozoic terrains, offering independent constraints for models of crustal evolution and supercontinent assembly (e.g., Nuna, Rodinia) that align with Condie's peaks and orogens framework.
proposals.novelty: While AFC modeling is standard, the systematic inversion to solve for the *assimilant*—rather than the degree of contamination or mantle source—is a non-obvious reversal of the standard problem. Applying this inverse approach at the scale of entire magmatic provinces to probe large-scale crustal architecture is a significant advance beyond case studies of single plutons.

ideas.title: Mineralogical Fingerprinting of the Baffin Root Using Multi-Modal Tomography
ideas.description: This research seed idea aims to determine the petrological cause of fast seismic velocities at depth below southern Baffin Island by drawing an analogy from materials science and non-destructive testing. In that field, the internal composition of complex, opaque composites is deciphered by correlating data from multiple techniques like ultrasonic mapping and XRD. Adapting this solution, the geological research will employ a multi-modal correlation framework. It will systematically integrate high-resolution seismic tomography (velocities, anisotropy) with petrological and geochemical data from mantle xenoliths (particularly from the North Atlantic Craton) and high-pressure experimental mineral physics. The outcome will be a calibrated, petrological inversion model that identifies the specific mineralogical assemblage (e.g., olivine Mg#, garnet and pyroxene modes), depletion history, and mantle fabric responsible for the observed seismic anomalies, providing a concrete explanation for the origin and nature of this deep cratonic root.

ideas.title: Quantifying the Primary Archean Component in Upper-Crustal Seismic Heterogeneity Through Multi-Proxy Forward Modeling
ideas.description: This research aims to resolve the origin of upper-crustal seismic variations in Archean terrains by developing an integrated, model-testing framework. It combines seismic tomography with independent geological and geochemical data from xenoliths and cratonic exposures to generate synthetic seismic models for a spectrum of scenarios ranging from purely primary to heavily overprinted crust. By using statistical inversion to find the best match between these synthetic models and observed seismic heterogeneity, the approach quantifies the relative likelihood and spatial footprint of preserved primary formation features. This moves the investigation beyond a simple binary question to a probabilistic mapping of primary Archean crustal architecture within its modified framework.

ideas.title: Unsupervised machine learning on a global Archean-Proterozoic basalt database to deconvolve and quantify secular mantle source evolution
ideas.description: This project constructs a rigorously curated global database of Archean to Paleoproterozoic basalt geochemistry. Unsupervised machine learning algorithms (e.g., non-negative matrix factorization, variational autoencoders) are then applied to this high-dimensional dataset to identify statistically robust end-member compositions without prior petrogenetic bias. The temporal evolution of the prevalence and geochemical signature of these learned end-members is tracked through the Archean-Proterozoic transition. This approach directly tests whether the 2 Ga Nb/Th shift represents a discrete change or a gradual evolution in mantle source components, while simultaneously discovering and characterizing the novel source reservoirs involved in early Earth differentiation.

ideas.title: Quantifying the petrological drivers of fast seismic velocities through integrated crustal thickening models for southern Baffin Island
ideas.description: This fused project integrates geophysical imaging with geodynamic-petrological modeling to establish a direct causal link between the process of crustal thickening and the resulting deep crustal composition. It will use the quantified magnitude and spatial pattern of Paleoproterozoic thickening from geological models to generate thermobarometric predictions for the resulting high-grade metamorphic and igneous assemblages. These predicted petrological columns are then translated into synthetic seismic velocity models, which are rigorously tested against the observed fast seismic anomalies. This approach moves beyond correlative description to a mechanistic understanding of how orogenic processes permanently modify deep crustal architecture and physical properties.

ideas.title: Imaging Paleo-Fluid Pathways to Constrain Early Earth Geodynamics
ideas.description: This research direction integrates magnetotelluric (MT) surveys with coupled geochemical-geodynamic models to investigate early Earth evolution. The MT data will image present-day crustal electrical conductivity anomalies, interpreted as fossil fluid pathways and metasomatic alteration from ancient hydrothermal and magmatic systems. These 3D conductivity structures provide ground-truth spatial constraints on past heat and mass transport, which are used to calibrate and validate models of early mantle convection and crustal growth. The synergy lies in using a modern geophysical snapshot to directly inform and test dynamic models of deep-time planetary processes, moving beyond indirect geochemical inferences.

ideas.title: Applying Forensic Signal Deconvolution to Characterize the Enriched Deep Mantle Component
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from forensic audio analysis, where mixed signals are deconvolved to identify individual sources, to the problem of characterizing the enriched deep mantle component. The analogous problem is isolating distinct voices from a noisy recording using known references and signal processing. The adapted research idea treats the geochemical array of mantle-derived rocks as a convoluted signal. By using known compositional end-members as reference signals and applying advanced statistical and inverse modeling techniques, the goal is to deconvolve the bulk mantle signal to isolate, quantify, and define the unique isotopic and elemental identity of the proposed early enriched component in the deep mantle, clarifying its origin distinct from later planetary differentiation processes.

ideas.title: Hierarchical Bayesian Joint Inversion for Mantle Source Compositions and Mixing Proportions
ideas.description: This method moves beyond simple mixing models by implementing a hierarchical Bayesian joint inversion that simultaneously solves for the isotopic and trace element signatures of mantle end-members (e.g., depleted MORB mantle, enriched components) and their proportional contributions to individual magma samples. The system is constrained by a global geochemical database of primitive basalts and uses weakly informative, physically bounded priors on end-member parameters. This approach directly quantifies and reduces prior-induced bias, yields robust posterior distributions with credible intervals for both source compositions and mixing proportions, and provides a statistically rigorous framework for testing hypotheses about mantle heterogeneity and the number of contributing components.

ideas.title: A Multi-Proxy Geophysical and Petrological Inversion to Determine the Origin of the Baffin Island Lithospheric Anomaly
ideas.description: This project will determine the cause of the fast seismic anomaly under southern Baffin Island by integrating multiple, independent geophysical observations into a unified inversion framework. Seismic tomography, anisotropy, and attenuation models will be combined with gravity, geoid, and magnetotelluric data to constrain temperature, composition, melt fraction, and fabric. These geophysical constraints will drive coupled geodynamic and thermodynamic phase equilibria models to predict petrological states that satisfy all datasets simultaneously, uniquely identifying whether the anomaly is caused by composition, temperature, depletion, or phase change.

ideas.title: Adapting Forensic Isotope Dilution for Nb/Th Chronometry
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from forensic toxicology to refine Nb/Th temporal trends in geochemistry. In forensics, double-spike isotope dilution mass spectrometry (IDMS) is used to achieve legally rigorous, ultra-precise quantification of trace analytes in complex biological matrices by correcting for both instrumental bias and sample preparation losses. By applying this analogous double-spike IDMS protocol to geological samples, we can add two enriched isotopes (e.g., ⁹⁴Nb and ²³⁰Th) as spikes prior to digestion. This adapted method will provide superior correction for mass fractionation and incomplete recovery during the challenging chemical separation of Nb and Th from silicate rocks, thereby yielding more accurate and precise Nb/Th ratios. The enhanced precision will allow for the detection of finer-scale temporal trends in mantle and crustal processes recorded in volcanic rocks and sediments, overcoming limitations posed by sample heterogeneity and low abundance.

ideas.title: Couple geochemical trends to mantle convection via tracer methods from computational fluid dynamics
ideas.description: This research expands the original seed idea by applying an analogical solution from Computational Fluid Dynamics. The analogous problem—tracking chemical species in a turbulent mixing process—is solved by integrating passive scalar tracers into a pre-computed or concurrently solved fluid velocity field. Adapted to early Earth geodynamics, the method involves seeding a mantle convection model (simulating early planetary cooling, magma ocean crystallization, or subsequent episodic overturn) with tracers representing distinct geochemical reservoirs. These tracers are carried by convection, and their concentrations in regions where melting occurs are used to predict the isotopic and elemental compositions of newly formed crust over time. The solution enables the coupling of high-resolution geodynamic models with complex geochemical systems without requiring a fully integrated thermodynamic-chemical solver, scaling the investigation of how mantle dynamics recorded themselves in the geochemical trends of early crustal rocks.

ideas.title: Probabilistic Multi-Resolution Architecture and Confidence Model of the Trans-Hudson Orogen
ideas.description: This project moves beyond a single deterministic 3D model to create a probabilistic, multi-resolution framework for the deep crustal architecture of the Trans-Hudson Orogen. It employs joint inversion and integration of all heterogeneous legacy and new targeted datasets within a Bayesian scheme, generating an ensemble of permissible 3D models. The core deliverable is a high-resolution 3D volume of interpretative confidence, explicitly quantifying where the architecture is well-constrained by data versus where it remains speculative. This approach provides a robust, transparent foundation for testing tectonic hypotheses and guiding future data acquisition by pinpointing the regions of greatest structural uncertainty.

ideas.title: A Multi-Sensor, Multi-Scale Tomographic Survey for 3D Crustal Architecture of the Trans-Hudson Orogen
ideas.description: This project adapts the solution from medical computed tomography (CT) to the challenge of imaging the deep crust. In medicine, a coherent 3D model is built by mathematically combining numerous 2D projections (X-rays) taken from many angles. The analogous geophysical solution is to conduct a large-scale, multi-parameter 'body scan' of the Trans-Hudson Orogen. This involves deploying a dense, permanent, and temporary array of seismic (active and passive), magnetotelluric, and gravity/magnetic sensors across the entire region. The diverse datasets, each sensitive to different rock properties (density, seismic velocity, resistivity), act as the equivalent of projections from different physical 'angles'. A sophisticated joint inversion framework will then integrate all data types to solve for a single, detailed 3D model of the crustal architecture, revealing boundaries, terrane sutures, underplated material, and the nature of the Moho with unprecedented clarity and consistency.

ideas.title: Joint probabilistic inversion of magnetotelluric and seismic data for quantitative crustal fluid characterization
ideas.description: This redesigned approach implements a unified inversion scheme that jointly models magnetotelluric impedance and seismic travel-time/attenuation data, constrained by a rock physics model linking electrical conductivity to seismic velocities. The probabilistic framework outputs ensembles of models quantifying the likelihood of fluid phase (e.g., aqueous vs. melt), salinity, and connectivity, directly addressing interpretation ambiguity. This transforms MT from a complementary imaging tool into a quantitative component of a multi-physics crustal fluid characterization system.

ideas.title: Adapting Topic Modeling from Text Analysis for Basalt Geochemistry
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from computational linguistics for discovering latent topics in text corpora to the problem of identifying novel source components in basalt geochemistry. The analogous solution uses unsupervised topic modeling algorithms like Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) to find underlying thematic structures. Adapted to geochemistry, each basalt sample is treated as a document, and its normalized elemental oxide or trace element concentrations are treated as words. By applying this probabilistic modeling framework to a large geochemical dataset, the method will uncover latent geochemical 'topics'—interpretable as distinct mantle reservoirs, crustal contaminants, or mixing end-members—and quantify their proportional contribution to each sample, offering a novel, data-driven approach to petrogenetic classification.

ideas.title: Anisotropy-Aware Ambient Noise Tomography with Joint Illumination-Inversion for Robust Crustal Imaging
ideas.description: This redesigned approach directly addresses the critical flaw of anisotropic noise fields by systematically characterizing and correcting for their influence. It begins with a diagnostic module that maps the directionality and persistence of noise sources across the seismic network. The core processing then uses advanced interferometric techniques, such as cross-correlation of cross-correlations or multidimensional deconvolution, to extract more accurate empirical Green's functions. Finally, a joint inversion framework simultaneously solves for subsurface velocity structure and noise source distribution parameters, yielding a more robust and artifact-suppressed crustal model with quantified illumination-related uncertainties.

ideas.title: Apply Medical MRI Reconstruction Techniques to Full Waveform Inversion for Crustal Imaging
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution to a parallel problem in medical imaging—specifically, MRI reconstruction via compressed sensing—to the challenge of obtaining high-resolution crustal velocity models through full waveform inversion (FWI). In MRI, compressed sensing enables high-fidelity image reconstruction from severely undersampled data by leveraging transform-domain sparsity. The analogous solution is adapted by reformulating the seismic FWI problem as a sparsity-promoting optimization. The objective is to find a crustal velocity model that minimizes the misfit between simulated and observed seismic waveforms while also minimizing the L1-norm of the model's representation in a domain (like curvelets) where geologic structures are sparse. This adaptation could significantly reduce the data acquisition density required for high-resolution FWI, mitigating its extreme computational cost and sensitivity to initial models, thereby making detailed crustal imaging more feasible.

ideas.title: Adapting Radio Astronomy's Aperture Synthesis to Seismic Ambient Noise Tomography
ideas.description: This idea adapts the core principle of aperture synthesis from radio astronomy to the problem of poor earthquake data coverage in seismology. In radio interferometry, signals from multiple small telescopes are combined to mimic a single giant telescope, filling in spatial gaps via Earth's rotation. The adapted solution applies this concept to seismic ambient noise tomography: a distributed network of seismometers is treated as elements of a synthetic aperture. By cross-correlating the continuous ambient seismic noise recorded across these stations over long time periods and applying interferometric principles, we can synthesize the wavefield that would have been recorded by a much larger, denser, or differently configured virtual array. This method would computationally enhance the resolving power and spatial coverage of existing sparse seismic networks, particularly in tectonically quiet or instrument-poor regions, leading to significantly improved crustal imaging without requiring a massive physical deployment of new stations.
